Stan code — Bias-adjusted model (Jung & Aloe 2026)

Source: vignettes/stan-bias-adjusted-jung.qmd

Model description

The Jung and Aloe (2026) bias-adjusted model extends Verde (2021) by replacing the composite risk-of-bias score with domain-specific binary RoB indicators. Each RoB domain contributes its own bias coefficient ξd\xi_d, allowing the model to estimate which domains are most strongly associated with effect inflation.

See Bias-adjusted models (Jung & Aloe 2026) for the full statistical rationale.

Mathematical specification

Decomposition:

yi=θi+bi+εi,εi∼𝒩(0,si2) y_i = \theta_i + b_i + \varepsilon_i, \quad \varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, s_i^2)

True effects:

θi∼𝒩(μ,τ2) \theta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \tau^2)

Bias component (domain-specific):

bi∼𝒩(∑d=1DRid⋅ξd,σb2) b_i \sim \mathcal{N}\!\left(\sum_{d=1}^{D} R_{id} \cdot \xi_d,\, \sigma_b^2\right)

where Rid∈{0,1}R_{id} \in \{0, 1\} is the RoB indicator for study ii in domain dd, and ξd\xi_d is the mean bias contribution from domain dd.

Priors:

μ∼𝒩(0,1),τ∼Half-Cauchy(0,0.5) \mu \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1), \qquad \tau \sim \text{Half-Cauchy}(0,\, 0.5)

ξd∼𝒩(0,0.5),d=1,…,D,σb∼Half-Normal(0,0.5) \xi_d \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\, 0.5), \quad d = 1, \ldots, D, \qquad \sigma_b \sim \text{Half-Normal}(0,\, 0.5)

Stan code 

data {
  int<lower=1> N;
  int<lower=1> K;
  int<lower=1> D;
  vector[N] y;
  vector<lower=0>[N] se;
  matrix[N, D] R;              // domain-specific RoB indicators
  array[N] int<lower=1> study;
}

parameters {
  real mu;
  real<lower=0> tau;
  vector[D] xi;
  real<lower=0> sigma_b;
  vector[K] z_theta;
  vector[N] z_b;
}

transformed parameters {
  vector[K] u_theta  = tau * z_theta;
  vector[N] b_mean   = R * xi;
  vector[N] b        = b_mean + sigma_b * z_b;
}

model {
  target += normal_lpdf(mu      | 0, 1);
  target += cauchy_lpdf(tau     | 0, 0.5);
  target += normal_lpdf(xi      | 0, 0.5);
  target += normal_lpdf(sigma_b | 0, 0.5);
  target += std_normal_lpdf(z_theta);
  target += std_normal_lpdf(z_b);

  target += normal_lpdf(y | mu + u_theta[study] + b, se);
}

generated quantities {
  real b_Intercept  = mu;
  vector[D] b_xi    = xi;
}

How bayesma calls this model 

bayesma(
  data,
  model_type      = "bias_corrected",
  bias_source     = "jung_aloe_2026",
  rob_domain_cols = c("rob_randomisation", "rob_deviations",
                      "rob_missing", "rob_measurement", "rob_selection")
)

rob_domain_cols names the binary RoB domain columns. Each should be coded 0 (low risk) or 1 (high risk).

Parameterisation notes

  • R * xi is a matrix-vector product: b_mean[i] = sum_d R[i,d] * xi[d].
  • A positive ξd\xi_d means high risk on domain dd is associated with upward bias.
  • b_xi in generated quantities provides the posterior for each domain coefficient.

Model comparison with Verde (2021)

The Jung & Aloe (2026) model has DD additional parameters compared to Verde (2021). Use compare_models() to assess whether domain-specific coefficients improve predictive accuracy:

compare_models(
  verde = fit_verde,
  jung  = fit_jung
)

In small meta-analyses (k<20k < 20), the additional parameters may not be estimable, and Verde (2021) is preferable.

Known sampling difficulties

Same as the Verde (2021) model. With many RoB domains (D>5D > 5) relative to studies (kk), the ξd\xi_d are weakly identified. Consider a group-level prior (horseshoe or regularised horseshoe) on ξd\xi_d for shrinkage towards zero.

On this page
